channel.title: Daily Digest
channel.description: Curated tech blog summaries, inspired by Andrej Karpathy's reading list

item.title: Value numbering
item.description: This article introduces value numbering as a compiler optimization technique that builds upon Static Single Assignment (SSA) form. It explains how identifying identical expressions allows compilers to eliminate redundant computations by reusing previously calculated values.

item.title: Wander Console 0.4.0
item.description: Wander Console 0.4.0 is a release of a decentralized, self-hosted web tool for exploring independent websites. The update introduces wildcard patterns for ignore lists, a 'via' query parameter for attribution, and improvements to the console picker algorithm.

item.title: Reading List 04/04/2026
item.description: This post provides a curated collection of industry updates and reading materials focusing on supply chain disruptions, infrastructure challenges, and emerging market trends. Topics include aluminum market issues, the impact of electric vehicles on traditional manufacturing hubs, and SpaceX's potential public offering.

item.title: What does Open Source mean?
item.description: This article explores the confusion surrounding the term 'open source,' noting that debates often fail because participants define it differently. It breaks down the term into four distinct categories: a licensing regime, a development methodology, a business model, and a supply chain.

item.title: The AI writing witchhunt is pointless.
item.description: The article argues against the backlash toward AI writing by comparing it to the historical collaborative model of Alexandre Dumas. It highlights that literary greats often relied on ghostwriters and assistants, suggesting that the current resistance to AI overlooks the long-standing nature of assisted writing.

item.title: Kalman and Bayes average grades
item.description: This article explains how calculating a rolling average grade serves as a foundational example for understanding Bayesian statistics and Kalman filtering. It demonstrates how incoming data points update previous estimates, highlighting the underlying mathematical connections between these concepts.

item.title: Absurd In Production
item.description: This post reflects on the five-month production experience of Absurd, a durable execution system built entirely on top of PostgreSQL. The author discusses system refinements, including enhanced claim handling, deadlock prevention, and improved workflow step management, confirming the viability of using SQL-based storage for long-running tasks.

item.title: Welcome to RSS Club!
item.description: The author introduces 'RSS Club', an exclusive series of blog posts accessible only to RSS/Atom subscribers. These posts are hidden from search engines and social media, creating a private space for long-form readers.

item.title: Loading... [13 kB]
item.description: This article explains how the Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) ensures data integrity when downloading files over the internet. It details the process of packet sequencing and acknowledgment to handle out-of-order delivery and packet loss effectively.

item.title: Pluralistic: EU ready to cave to Trump on tech (04 Apr 2026)
item.source: Pluralistic: Daily links from Cory Doctorow
item.description: Cory Doctorow critiques the EU's potential failure to regulate US tech giants amidst geopolitical pressures under the Trump administration. The piece argues that the EU should capitalize on current crises to address American digital surveillance, monopolistic data practices, and the weaponization of software against public institutions.

item.title: Quoting Kyle Daigle
item.description: This post highlights GitHub's rapid platform growth, citing data from COO Kyle Daigle. It specifically notes a massive increase in commit activity and the doubling of GitHub Actions usage minutes.

item.title: Vulnerability Research Is Cooked
item.description: Thomas Ptacek argues that AI coding agents are poised to revolutionize vulnerability research by automating exploit discovery. By leveraging vast pattern-matching capabilities and relentless search processes, these models can identify zero-day vulnerabilities more efficiently than traditional methods.

item.title: The cognitive impact of coding agents
item.description: Simon Willison shares a viral short-form video clip from his appearance on Lenny Rachitsky's podcast. The post explores the cognitive impact of using coding agents and touches on the broader implications of AI engineering.

item.title: The Mystery of Rennes-le-Château, Part 3: A Secret History
item.description: This article explores the historical and pseudo-historical origins of Rennes-le-Château, focusing on the investigation by Henry Lincoln. It serves as part of a series examining the real-world research that inspired the game Gabriel Knight 3.

item.title: The Reckoning
item.description: The author reflects on the societal 'reckoning' brought about by AI, criticizing how its marketing has focused on existential threats and displacement rather than utility. The post contrasts the hyper-individualism of the US with the communal cohesion observed in Hong Kong, arguing for structural improvements to restore social trust.

item.title: Em Dashes: Back In Style?
item.source: Tedium: The Dull Side of the Internet.
item.description: The author reflects on their long-term struggle to maintain a massive, legacy WordPress blog archive. They explore Cloudflare's new 'EmDash' tool, which aims to simplify migrating unstable WordPress sites to the modern Astro framework.

item.title: Package Manager Easter Eggs
item.description: This article explores the long-standing tradition of hidden easter eggs within various software package managers. It showcases specific examples, such as the 'moo' commands in apt, aptitude, and zypper, which reveal creative ASCII art and playful interactions.

item.title: The "Passive Income" trap ate a generation of entrepreneurs
item.description: The article critiques the trend of entrepreneurs pursuing 'passive income' through low-value ventures like dropshipping. It suggests that many such business models lack genuine innovation and long-term sustainability.

item.title: Roman moon, Greek moon
item.description: The article explains the terminology used for the closest approach to the moon, noting the interchangeability of 'perilune' and 'periselene'. It clarifies that the terms are derived from Roman and Greek roots for the moon, respectively.

item.title: Hyperbolic version of Napier’s mnemonic
item.description: The author explores a hyperbolic analog to Napier's mnemonic, a tool traditionally used in spherical trigonometry. While noting that hyperbolic analogs exist for most geometric concepts, the author highlights this specific discovery as a unique mathematical curiosity.

item.title: How can I use Read­Directory­ChangesW to know when someone is copying a file out of the directory?
item.description: This post clarifies that file copying is not a distinct file system operation, making it impossible to detect directly via ReadDirectoryChangesW. It explains that since copying is just a series of read and write operations, the file system cannot distinguish it from other file access patterns.

item.title: Book Review: Superintelligence - Paths, Dangers, Strategies by Nick Bostrom ★★★★⯪
item.description: This review discusses Nick Bostrom's 2014 book, Superintelligence, highlighting its profound analysis of the existential risks associated with true artificial intelligence. The author emphasizes the book's foresight in outlining necessary safety measures and conceptual frameworks long before current AI advancements.

item.title: My Zip bomb strategy is not as effective as it used to be
item.description: The author discusses the declining effectiveness of using zip bombs to neutralize malicious web bots. While previously a reliable defense for a modest server setup, the author notes that modern, more sophisticated bots are increasingly able to circumvent this mitigation strategy.

item.title: Quoting Willy Tarreau
item.description: Willy Tarreau reports a massive increase in Linux kernel security vulnerability reports, rising from a few per week to 5-10 per day. This surge is largely attributed to AI tools, which have improved detection efficiency to the point of generating frequent duplicate findings.

item.title: Quoting Daniel Stenberg
item.description: Daniel Stenberg, the lead developer of cURL, discusses the changing impact of AI on open source security. He notes a shift from an influx of AI-generated 'slop' to a high volume of legitimate, high-quality security reports that require significant manual review.

item.title: Quoting Greg Kroah-Hartman
item.description: Linux kernel maintainer Greg Kroah-Hartman notes a significant shift in AI-generated security reports. Initially dismissed as low-quality 'slop,' AI tools are now producing accurate and actionable security analysis for open-source projects.

item.title: Can JavaScript Escape a CSP Meta Tag Inside an Iframe?
item.description: This post investigates whether JavaScript can bypass Content Security Policy (CSP) meta tags injected within sandboxed iframes. The author demonstrates that these meta tags are effectively enforced by the browser even when subsequent untrusted JavaScript attempts to modify them.

item.title: The Axios supply chain attack used individually targeted social engineering
item.description: Axios maintainers released a postmortem detailing a sophisticated supply chain attack that targeted a developer through highly personalized social engineering. The attackers impersonated company leadership and used fake professional environments to trick the maintainer into installing malware during a video conference.

item.title: Build your own Dial-up ISP with a Raspberry Pi
item.description: This article explores the technical history of the iBook G3 and its significance as the first consumer laptop with built-in Wi-Fi. It sets the stage for a project involving legacy hardware connectivity and the creation of a dial-up ISP using a Raspberry Pi.

item.title: Programming (with AI agents) as theory building
item.description: This article explores the concept of programming not as mere instruction-giving, but as the active construction of mental models and theories. It suggests that integrating AI agents into the development process shifts the developer's role toward architecting these theoretical frameworks.

item.title: SQLAlchemy 2 In Practice - Chapter 3 - One-To-Many Relationships
item.description: This article serves as the third chapter of Miguel Grinberg's book on SQLAlchemy 2, focusing on implementing one-to-many relationships. It follows a previous discussion on product queries, transitioning into managing data associations between entities.

item.title: The Blandness of Systematic Rules vs. The Delight of Localized Sensitivity
item.description: This post explores the tension between adhering to rigid systematic UI design rules and the value of localized, context-aware design choices. The author argues that while systematic rules promote consistency, thoughtful exceptions can provide a level of delight and clarity that universal guidelines sometimes stifle, especially as software scales.

item.title: Information and Technological Evolution
item.description: This article explores the nature of technological progress and critiques the quality of existing literature on the subject. It highlights the author's ongoing interest in understanding how information and technology evolve over time.

item.title: Time is a User-Interface
item.description: This article explores the concept of time as a fundamental component of user interface design. It examines how temporal dynamics and sequencing influence user experience and interaction patterns.

item.title: Artemis II, Apollo 8, and Apollo 13
item.description: The article compares the Artemis II mission to the historic Apollo 8 and Apollo 13 missions. It highlights similarities in mission objectives, specifically the lunar flyby trajectory used to prepare for future moon landings.

item.title: Why doesn’t the system let you declare your own messages to have the same semantics as WM_COPY­DATA?
item.description: This post explains why developers cannot replicate the unique cross-process memory semantics of WM_COPYDATA for custom Windows messages. It highlights that the system imposes strict limitations to ensure security and prevent memory corruption during inter-process communication.

item.title: Concert Review: London Philharmonic - Pictures at an Exhibition ★★★★★
item.description: This blog post provides a personal review of a London Philharmonic concert featuring works by Mark-Anthony Turnage and Béla Bartók. The author highlights the emotional intensity and chaotic nature of the performance, specifically praising the inventive delivery of 'Three Screaming Popes'.

item.title: Random File Format
item.description: The author explores a concept for a resilient file format capable of playing back content even if the file is incomplete or corrupted. This idea was inspired by common issues with truncated media downloads during the early 2000s.

item.title: Pluralistic: It's extremely good that Claude's source-code leaked (02 Apr 2026)
item.source: Pluralistic: Daily links from Cory Doctorow
item.description: Cory Doctorow discusses the recent leak of Anthropic's Claude Code due to a configuration error and critiques the company's use of DMCA takedown notices. The article highlights the legal complexities of copyright law, particularly how strict liability impacts intermediaries and platforms.

item.title: 13th Year of Blogging
item.description: The author reflects on thirteen years of blogging, highlighting the challenges of technical maintenance and the importance of perseverance. They argue that personal experience and long-term consistency create a unique value that AI-generated content cannot replicate.

item.title: Spring 2026 Pebble App Contest + SDK Updates
item.description: Eric Migicovsky has announced the Spring 2026 Pebble App Contest to celebrate the start of mass production for the Pebble Time 2. The post invites developers to participate while highlighting upcoming updates to the Pebble SDK.

item.title: Highlights from my conversation about agentic engineering on Lenny's Podcast
item.description: Simon Willison shares highlights from his guest appearance on Lenny's Podcast, where he discusses the current state of AI and agentic engineering. The post covers key topics including the AI inflection point, the evolving role of software engineers, and the rise of automated 'dark factories'.

item.title: Gemma 4: Byte for byte, the most capable open models
item.description: Google has released Gemma 4, a series of open-weights vision-capable models ranging from 2B to 31B parameters, featuring a novel Per-Layer Embedding technique to enhance efficiency. Simon Willison provides a brief technical overview of the architecture and shares his hands-on experience testing these models via LM Studio.

item.title: llm-gemini 0.30
item.description: Simon Willison has released version 0.30 of the llm-gemini tool. This update adds support for new Google models including gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview and the Gemma 4 family.

item.title: March 2026 sponsors-only newsletter
item.description: Simon Willison has released his March 2026 sponsors-only newsletter, covering topics such as agentic engineering, local LLM streaming on Mac, and recent software supply chain security issues. This monthly publication offers exclusive insights and technical updates for his GitHub sponsors.

item.title: datasette-llm 0.1a6
item.description: The datasette-llm 0.1a6 release introduces a quality-of-life update where default models are automatically added to the allowed models list. It also includes improved documentation for the tool's Python API usage.

item.title: datasette-enrichments-llm 0.2a1
item.description: Simon Willison has released version 0.2a1 of the datasette-enrichments-llm plugin. This update introduces the passing of the 'actor' parameter to the llm.mode method when triggering an enrichment.

item.title: On the Vergecast, On Video
item.description: Anil Dash reflects on his guest appearance on The Vergecast, where he discussed the importance of keeping podcasting an open ecosystem. He also highlights a segment featuring Jason Snell, who provides a critical analysis of Apple's 50-year history and its recent moral shortcomings.

item.title: Mini-review: David Pogue's Apple: The First 50 Years
item.description: This review evaluates David Pogue's book, 'Apple: The First 50 Years,' comparing it to previous historical accounts like 'Apple Confidential.' The reviewer finds that, despite initial skepticism, the book provides a surprisingly deep technical dive into Apple's early history, including the Apple-1, Apple III, and the development of the Macintosh.

item.title: Summary of reading: January - March 2026
item.description: This blog post provides a quarterly digest of book summaries covering various topics including political theory, geopolitics, primatology, and martial arts history. The author offers brief critical reviews, assessing the value and limitations of each work.

item.title: April Cools Post: New York vs Chicago Pizza
item.description: This post discusses the ongoing debate between Chicago deep-dish and New York-style pizza, arguing that the comparison is fundamentally flawed. The author shares personal insights on Chicago's culinary identity, distinguishing between authentic deep-dish and the common 'stuffed' pizza style.

item.title: Mar '26 Notes
item.description: This post summarizes the author's March 2026 exploration of algebraic graph theory through academic textbooks. It also chronicles the spontaneous creation and unexpected growth of 'Wander Console,' an open-source tool for discovering independent websites.

item.title: Wayne’s World
item.source: Tedium: The Dull Side of the Internet.
item.description: This article profiles Ronald G. Wayne, the often-overlooked third co-founder of Apple who sold his stake after only two weeks. It explores his multifaceted life beyond Apple, highlighting his career as a polymath, artist, and his work at Atari.

item.title: The Entire Internet Is a UGC Reaction Video Now
item.description: The article explores the proliferation of user-generated reaction videos and bizarre websites, framing them as signs of a fracturing digital culture. It reflects on the feeling of social decay found within these internet artifacts.

item.title: Say the Thing You Want
item.description: This post highlights the importance of clearly articulating professional needs and desires rather than relying on silence. It argues that avoiding direct communication is counterproductive and urges readers to speak up to achieve their goals.

item.title: Notes from March 2026
item.description: Evan Hahn recounts his busy March 2026, highlighting his writings on software errors, AI disclosure, and personal projects. The post also details his professional work at Ghost, contributions to Zelda Dungeon, and curated links regarding current AI trends.

item.title: Pentagonal numbers are truncated triangular numbers
item.description: This post explains the geometric relationship between pentagonal numbers and triangular numbers. It demonstrates how pentagonal number diagrams can be visually transformed into specific triangular number patterns.

item.title: The cover of C++: The Programming Language raises questions not answered by the cover
item.description: This post discusses the iconic cover art of Bjarne Stroustrup's book, 'C++: The Programming Language.' It highlights the lack of clear context or explanation for the imagery used on the book's cover.

item.title: Claude Code won April Fools Day this year
item.description: Xe Iaso highlights Claude Code's successful April Fools' Day prank, which featured a digital pet (Tamagotchi) experience. The post playfully praises the creative execution of the feature as a standout moment for the holiday.

item.title: GopherTree
item.description: The author discusses the Gopher protocol's structure and presents a custom minimalist Gopher client written in C. The client emphasizes a directory tree interface, effectively hiding non-standard 'informational selectors' to improve navigation and user focus.

item.title: Pluralistic: Trumpismo vs minilateralism (01 Apr 2026)
item.source: Pluralistic: Daily links from Cory Doctorow
item.description: This post explores the tension between traditional US-led globalism and the rise of 'Trumpismo,' arguing that previous international orders relied on American dominance disguised as neutral cooperation. It highlights how the US historically benefited from controlling infrastructure, currency, and data, while current political shifts threaten these systemic advantages.

item.title: What is Copilot exactly?
item.description: The author recounts their attempt to improve productivity using Microsoft Copilot after being inspired by a colleague. After successfully creating automated workflows for administrative tasks, they discovered the confusion stemmed from using different versions of the tool, as their colleague was referring to the code editor version rather than the workplace assistant.

item.title: datasette-extract 0.3a0
item.description: Datasette-extract version 0.3a0 has been released, featuring an integration with datasette-llm for model configuration. Users can now utilize the 'extract' purpose to manage available AI models for data extraction tasks.

item.title: datasette-enrichments-llm 0.2a0
item.description: The datasette-enrichments-llm plugin has been updated to version 0.2a0. This release integrates with datasette-llm to allow users to configure and manage specific AI models for data enrichment tasks.

item.title: datasette-llm-usage 0.2a0
item.description: Datasette-llm-usage version 0.2a0 introduces key architectural changes, offloading pricing features to a separate plugin and requiring datasette-llm for model configuration. It also adds optional detailed logging for prompts and tool calls and updates security permissions for the simple prompt page.

item.title: datasette-llm 0.1a5
item.description: Datasette-llm version 0.1a5 has been released, introducing a key update to the llm_prompt_context() plugin hook. This improvement enables the tracking of prompts within chains, specifically facilitating the monitoring of tool call loops.

item.title: Quoting Soohoon Choi
item.description: Soohoon Choi argues that economic incentives will drive AI to produce high-quality, maintainable code rather than low-quality 'slop.' Competitive market pressures will favor models that enable developers to build reliable software quickly and efficiently.

item.title: Supply Chain Attack on Axios Pulls Malicious Dependency from npm
item.description: The popular Axios npm package was compromised after a leaked token allowed attackers to inject a malicious dependency, plain-crypto-js, into recent versions. The attack highlights the risks of supply chain vulnerabilities and the necessity of adopting trusted publishing to prevent unauthorized package releases.

item.title: DRAM pricing is killing the hobbyist SBC market
item.description: Raspberry Pi has announced significant price increases for its high-RAM Single Board Computers (SBCs) due to rising DRAM costs. The updates include a new 3GB model and a substantial hike for the 16GB Pi 5, reflecting broader market trends affecting hobbyist hardware.

item.title: Telnyx, LiteLLM and Axios: the supply chain crisis
Solving Yesterday’s Problems Will Kill You
Before you check if an update caused your problem, check that it wasn’t a problem before the update
My ramblings are available over gopher
Pluralistic: State Dems must stop ICE from stealing the midterms (31 Mar 2026)
item.source: Pluralistic: Daily links from Cory Doctorow

item.title: The Webs Digital Locks have Never had a Stronger Opponent
HIBP Mega Update: Passkeys, k-Anonymity Searches, Massive Speed Enhancements and a Bulk Domain Verification API
Notes on going solo: celebrating 6 years of Studio Self
A question about the maximimum number of values in a registry key raises questions about the question
Pluralistic: Market participation is exhausting (30 Mar 2026)
item.source: Pluralistic: Daily links from Cory Doctorow
